title: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Sinoatrial Node
authors: William H. Sauer, Bruce A. Koplan

items.subtopic: The Bradyarrhythmias: Disorders of the Sinoatrial Node | STRUCTURE AND PHYSIOLOGY OF THE SA NODE
items.title: Understanding Sinus Node Dysfunction and Its Management
items.content: Sinus node dysfunction occurs when the natural pacemaker of the heart, the sinoatrial (SA) node, fails to function properly. This can lead to symptoms like fatigue, exercise intolerance, or dyspnea due to bradycardia. Diagnostic evaluation involves identifying reversible causes and confirming sinus node dysfunction via telemetry monitoring or exercise testing. If structural heart disease is suspected, cardiac imaging may be necessary. Permanent pacemaker implantation becomes essential for long-term management of symptomatic bradycardia when irreversible sinus node dysfunction is confirmed.
keyPoints: Sinus node dysfunction symptoms: fatigue, exercise intolerance, dyspnea
Diagnostic evaluation includes telemetry monitoring or exercise testing
Permanent pacemaker implantation is the definitive treatment for symptomatic bradycardia due to irreversible sinus node dysfunction
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Sinoatrial Node · The Bradyarrhythmias: Disorders of the Sinoatrial Node | STRUCTURE AND PHYSIOLOGY OF THE SA NODE

items.subtopic: The Bradyarrhythmias: Disorders of the Sinoatrial Node | STRUCTURE AND PHYSIOLOGY OF THE SA NODE
items.title: Physiological Characteristics and Autonomic Regulation of the SA Node
items.content: The SA node, located at the right atrial-superior vena cava junction, is a complex structure with specialized pacemaker cells. These cells have fewer myofibrils and no T tubules compared to surrounding myocardium. The intrinsic heart rate in denervated hearts is approximately 100 beats/min due to lack of parasympathetic tone. Autonomic regulation plays a significant role; at baseline, the SA node has predominant parasympathetic innervation, which can be overridden by sympathetic stimulation or removal of parasympathetic influence.
keyPoints: SA nodal artery arises from right coronary artery in 55–60% and left circumflex artery in 40–45%
Intrinsic heart rate is approximately 100 beats/min in denervated hearts
Resting heart rate in patients <85 years influenced by parasympathetic tone, intrinsic heart rate 100–110 beats/min
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Sinoatrial Node · The Bradyarrhythmias: Disorders of the Sinoatrial Node | STRUCTURE AND PHYSIOLOGY OF THE SA NODE

items.subtopic: The Bradyarrhythmias: Disorders of the Sinoatrial Node | STRUCTURE AND PHYSIOLOGY OF THE SA NODE
items.title: Age-Related Changes in Heart Rate Regulation by the SA Node
items.content: As individuals age, there is a progressive decline in maximum heart rate, approximately 5–6 beats/min per decade. Despite this decline, resting heart rates typically remain unchanged due to a gradual decrease in parasympathetic tone and an increase in sympathetic influence by the ninth decade.
keyPoints: Maximum heart rate declines 5–6 beats/min for each decade of age
Resting heart rate remains constant with age due to decreased parasympathetic tone
Predominant sympathetic tone becomes more influential by the ninth decade
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Sinoatrial Node · The Bradyarrhythmias: Disorders of the Sinoatrial Node | STRUCTURE AND PHYSIOLOGY OF THE SA NODE

items.title: Role of Electrocardiographic Recording in Diagnosing SND
items.content: Electrocardiographic recording is central to diagnosing and managing sinus node dysfunction (SND). It helps correlate symptoms like fatigue, exercise intolerance, or syncope with slow heart rate or pauses. Baseline ECG can detect sinus bradycardia but may not always link it to symptoms. Longer-term recordings via Holter monitors or mobile telemetry are useful for symptom correlation.
keyPoints: Electrocardiographic recording is crucial for diagnosing SND.
Baseline ECG detects sinus bradycardia but may lack symptom correlation.
Longer-term monitoring (Holter, mobile telemetry) helps correlate symptoms with rate abnormalities.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Sinoatrial Node · DIAGNOSIS OF SA NODAL DISEASE

items.title: Evaluating Reversible Causes of SND
items.content: Once SND is evident, reversible causes such as hypothyroidism and rate-slowing medications should be ruled out. Sleep apnea, a common cause, can lead to sinus bradycardia and pauses during sleep due to high vagal tone. Asymptomatic sinus bradycardia during sleep typically does not require pacing.
keyPoints: Rule out reversible causes like hypothyroidism and rate-slowing medications.
Suspect sleep apnea if marked sinus bradycardia occurs during sleep.
Asymptomatic sinus bradycardia during sleep usually doesn't need pacing.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Sinoatrial Node · DIAGNOSIS OF SA NODAL DISEASE

items.title: Use of Invasive Electrophysiology Testing in SND
items.content: Invasive electrophysiology testing (EPS) is rarely used solely for assessing sinus node function. It may be considered during EPS for other indications or when the diagnosis remains uncertain after noninvasive evaluation. EPS can assess sinus node recovery time (SNRT), sinoatrial conduction time (SACT), and intrinsic heart rate using pharmacologic blockade. However, abnormal SNRT or SACT alone does not indicate permanent pacing.
keyPoints: EPS is rarely used solely for assessing SND.
Consider EPS during other studies or if diagnosis remains uncertain post noninvasive evaluation.
Intrinsic heart rate can be assessed with intravenous propranolol and atropine.
Abnormal SNRT or SACT alone does not indicate permanent pacing.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Sinoatrial Node · DIAGNOSIS OF SA NODAL DISEASE

items.title: Understanding Sinoatrial Exit Block Patterns
items.content: Sinoatrial exit block can be categorized similarly to atrioventricular node blocks, with Type I involving fixed delay and Type II showing progressive delay or intermittent failure. Type II second-degree SA block is identified on ECG when the sinus rate abruptly halves.
keyPoints: Type I SA block involves a fixed delay out of the sinus node.
Type II SA block can show progressive delay (Mobitz I) or fixed delay with intermittent failure (Mobitz II).
Type II second-degree SA block is inferred when the sinus rate abruptly transitions to half the previous rate on ECG.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Sinoatrial Node · SA NODAL DYSFUNCTION SUBTYPES

items.title: Tachy-Brady Syndrome and Pacemaker Consideration
items.content: Tachy-brady syndrome, a subset of sick sinus syndrome, involves alternating high heart rates (often atrial fibrillation) with symptomatic bradycardia or pauses. Medications for rate control can worsen bradycardia, making pacemaker implantation a consideration.
keyPoints: Tachy-brady syndrome includes high heart rates and symptomatic bradycardia.
Medications for tachycardia may exacerbate bradycardia episodes.
Pacemaker implantation is often considered due to medication effects.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Sinoatrial Node · SA NODAL DYSFUNCTION SUBTYPES

items.title: Chronotropic Incompetence and Its Impact on Exercise Tolerance
items.content: Chronotropic incompetence (CI) refers to the heart's inability to increase its rate during activity, contributing significantly to exercise intolerance. CI can be defined by failure to reach a percentage of age-predicted maximal heart rate or other criteria.
keyPoints: CI is the inability of the heart to increase rate with demand.
CI contributes more to increased oxygen uptake than stroke volume during exercise.
Definitions include failing to achieve 85% of (220 - age) × 0.85 or a maximum heart rate of 208 - (0.7 × age).
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Sinoatrial Node · SA NODAL DYSFUNCTION SUBTYPES

items.subtopic: The Bradyarrhythmias: Disorders of the Sinoatrial Node | STRUCTURE AND PHYSIOLOGY OF THE SA NODE
items.question: Which artery supplies the sinoatrial (SA) node in the majority of individuals?
items.explanation: The SA nodal artery arises from the right coronary artery in 55–60% of persons.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Sinoatrial Node · The Bradyarrhythmias: Disorders of the Sinoatrial Node | STRUCTURE AND PHYSIOLOGY OF THE SA NODE

items.subtopic: The Bradyarrhythmias: Disorders of the Sinoatrial Node | STRUCTURE AND PHYSIOLOGY OF THE SA NODE
items.question: What is the primary mechanism for phase 0 depolarization in SA node myocytes?
items.explanation: The upstroke of depolarization in SA node myocytes is mediated by calcium rather than sodium current.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Sinoatrial Node · The Bradyarrhythmias: Disorders of the Sinoatrial Node | STRUCTURE AND PHYSIOLOGY OF THE SA NODE

items.subtopic: The Bradyarrhythmias: Disorders of the Sinoatrial Node | STRUCTURE AND PHYSIOLOGY OF THE SA NODE
items.question: What characterizes electrical diastole in nodal cells?
items.explanation: Electrical diastole in nodal cells is characterized by slow diastolic depolarization (phase 4).
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Sinoatrial Node · The Bradyarrhythmias: Disorders of the Sinoatrial Node | STRUCTURE AND PHYSIOLOGY OF THE SA NODE

items.subtopic: The Bradyarrhythmias: Disorders of the Sinoatrial Node | STRUCTURE AND PHYSIOLOGY OF THE SA NODE
items.question: What happens to the intrinsic heart rate after atropine administration?
items.explanation: The absence or elimination of autonomic influence on the SA node after atropine administration leads to an intrinsic heart rate normally 100–110 beats/min.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Sinoatrial Node · The Bradyarrhythmias: Disorders of the Sinoatrial Node | STRUCTURE AND PHYSIOLOGY OF THE SA NODE

items.question: Which of the following devices can be used to correlate symptoms with rate abnormalities in patients suspected of having sinus node dysfunction?
options: Holter monitors and mobile cardiac telemetry
items.explanation: Longer-term recording with Holter monitors or mobile cardiac telemetry can help correlate symptoms with rate abnormalities.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Sinoatrial Node · DIAGNOSIS OF SA NODAL DISEASE

items.question: What is a common reversible cause of sinus node disease that should be suspected if marked sinus bradycardia and prolonged sinus pauses are observed during sleep?
items.explanation: Sleep apnea is a common reversible cause of sinus node disease that can manifest with marked sinus bradycardia and prolonged pauses during sleep.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Sinoatrial Node · DIAGNOSIS OF SA NODAL DISEASE

items.question: Which test is used to evaluate potential cardiac abnormalities associated with suspected structural heart disease in patients with sinus node dysfunction?
items.explanation: Transthoracic echocardiography is used to detect potential cardiac abnormalities associated with sinus node dysfunction.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Sinoatrial Node · DIAGNOSIS OF SA NODAL DISEASE

items.question: What is the role of invasive electrophysiology testing in evaluating sinus node function?
options: It is routinely used to assess all patients with suspected SND.
It is rarely utilized beyond noninvasive techniques and not indicated for asymptomatic patients.
It is the primary method for diagnosing sinus bradycardia.
It provides absolute indications for permanent pacing based on SNRT or SACT.
items.explanation: Invasive electrophysiology testing is rarely utilized beyond noninvasive techniques and not indicated for asymptomatic patients with sinus bradycardia.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Sinoatrial Node · DIAGNOSIS OF SA NODAL DISEASE

items.question: Which type of sinoatrial (SA) block is characterized by a fixed delay out of the sinus node?
options: Type II SA block, Mobitz I
Type II SA block, Mobitz II
items.explanation: Type I SA block involves a fixed delay out of the sinus node.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Sinoatrial Node · SA NODAL DYSFUNCTION SUBTYPES

items.question: What is inferred on an ECG if the sinus rate abruptly transitions to half the previous rate?
options: Type II SA block, Mobitz I
Type II SA block, Mobitz II
items.explanation: This pattern is indicative of Type II second-degree SA block.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Sinoatrial Node · SA NODAL DYSFUNCTION SUBTYPES

items.question: What condition involves high heart rates alternating with symptomatic bradycardia or pauses, often leading to pacemaker implantation consideration?
items.explanation: Tachycardia-bradycardia syndrome involves alternating high and low heart rates.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Sinoatrial Node · SA NODAL DYSFUNCTION SUBTYPES

items.question: Which condition is defined as the inability of the heart to increase its rate to meet activity or demand?
items.explanation: Chronotropic incompetence is the inability of the heart to increase its rate appropriately.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Sinoatrial Node · SA NODAL DYSFUNCTION SUBTYPES

items.subtopic: The Bradyarrhythmias: Disorders of the Sinoatrial Node | STRUCTURE AND PHYSIOLOGY OF THE SA NODE
items.statement: SA node cells have intercalated disks visible on light microscopy.
items.explanation: SA nodal cells do not have intercalated disks visible on light microscopy.

items.statement: Intrinsic heart rate declines with age.
items.explanation: Intrinsic heart rate declines 5–6 beats/min for each decade of age.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Sinoatrial Node · The Bradyarrhythmias: Disorders of the Sinoatrial Node | STRUCTURE AND PHYSIOLOGY OF THE SA NODE

items.statement: Asymptomatic sinus bradycardia and pauses during sleep are typically an indication for pacing.
items.explanation: Asymptomatic sinus bradycardia and pauses during sleep are not typically an indication for pacing.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Sinoatrial Node · DIAGNOSIS OF SA NODAL DISEASE

items.statement: Advanced cardiac imaging is indicated for evaluating possible myocardial diseases such as amyloidosis in patients with suspected SND.
items.explanation: Advanced cardiac imaging is used to evaluate potential myocardial diseases like amyloidosis associated with sinus node dysfunction.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Sinoatrial Node · DIAGNOSIS OF SA NODAL DISEASE

items.statement: Sinus node fibrosis is a normal part of aging and does not always result in sick sinus syndrome.
items.explanation: Increased fibrosis and decreased pacemaker cells are normal with age, but SND remains pathologic.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Sinoatrial Node · SA NODAL DYSFUNCTION SUBTYPES

items.statement: Invasive electrophysiology testing is typically necessary to determine the type of SA block for management purposes.
items.explanation: The exercise of determining the type of SA block with invasive EPS is not usually necessary as it does not alter management.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Sinoatrial Node · SA NODAL DYSFUNCTION SUBTYPES

items.subtopic: The Bradyarrhythmias: Disorders of the Sinoatrial Node | STRUCTURE AND PHYSIOLOGY OF THE SA NODE
items.assertion: SA node cells have a less negative resting membrane potential compared with atrial or ventricular myocytes.
items.reason: This is due to the complement of ionic currents present in nodal cells.
items.explanation: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Sinoatrial Node · The Bradyarrhythmias: Disorders of the Sinoatrial Node | STRUCTURE AND PHYSIOLOGY OF THE SA NODE

items.subtopic: The Bradyarrhythmias: Disorders of the Sinoatrial Node | STRUCTURE AND PHYSIOLOGY OF THE SA NODE
items.assertion: SA node myocytes have fast sodium channels.
items.reason: These channels are responsible for phase 0 depolarization in nodal cells.
items.explanation: A is false, but R is true; SA node cells lack fast sodium channels.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Sinoatrial Node · The Bradyarrhythmias: Disorders of the Sinoatrial Node | STRUCTURE AND PHYSIOLOGY OF THE SA NODE

items.assertion: Standard Bruce protocol treadmill testing may not detect chronotropic incompetence during submaximal exercise.
items.reason: More gradual effort increases in treadmill protocols can reveal abnormalities of rate increase during submaximal exercise.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why standard Bruce protocol may not detect certain types of chronotropic incompetence.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Sinoatrial Node · DIAGNOSIS OF SA NODAL DISEASE

items.assertion: Invasive electrophysiology testing is widely used to assess sinus node function in all patients with suspected SND.
items.reason: EPS can determine SNRT and SACT, which are absolute indications for permanent pacing.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because invasive electrophysiology testing is rarely utilized beyond noninvasive techniques; the reason is also false as abnormal SNRT or SACT alone do not provide absolute indications for permanent pacing.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Sinoatrial Node · DIAGNOSIS OF SA NODAL DISEASE

items.assertion: Sinus bradycardia in acute myocardial infarction can be exacerbated by increased vagal tone.
items.reason: Increased vagal tone, such as through the Bezold-Jarisch reflex, contributes to sinus bradycardia during an MI.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Sinoatrial Node · SA NODAL DYSFUNCTION SUBTYPES

items.assertion: Type II SA block can be inferred when there is progressive shortening of the P-P interval leading to a sinus pause.
items.reason: This pattern is characteristic of Sinoatrial Wenckebach, which involves progressive prolongation of SA conduction.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because Type II SA block does not involve progressive shortening of the P-P interval; this describes Sinoatrial Wenckebach.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Sinoatrial Node · SA NODAL DYSFUNCTION SUBTYPES
